meaning: The all-purpose adjective form of 'kir' (penis). Anything terrible, worthless, or pathetic can be 'kiri' — a kiri car, a kiri movie, a kiri day. It transforms the penis from a body part into a quality rating system where everything associated with it is garbage. One of the most frequently used adjectives in vulgar Persian.
cultural_context: The 'kir' root is to Persian profanity what 'fuck' is to English — it generates dozens of derivatives. 'Kiri' as an adjective has become so common among younger Iranians that it barely registers as vulgar in casual conversation between friends. In the diaspora (LA, Toronto, London), it's essentially standard slang. Older Iranians still find it shocking.
example_usage: Said about anything disappointing: 'In film kheili kiri bood' — this movie was really shitty. Used between friends, never in formal settings or around elders.

regional_variations.note: So common it's barely vulgar among under-30s
Standard slang in Persian-speaking communities abroad

meaning: One of Persian's nuclear insults — literally 'one who drags kos (vagina),' meaning a pimp. But nobody processes the literal meaning; it functions as a general-purpose extreme insult equivalent to 'motherfucker' or 'piece of shit.' Calling someone 'koskesh' is declaring them beneath contempt.
cultural_context: In a culture where family honor and female chastity are deeply intertwined, implying someone pimps out women (implicitly their own family) is devastating. This word can genuinely provoke violence in Iran. In diaspora communities, particularly among younger Persians in LA and Toronto, it's been somewhat deflated through overuse — still vulgar but less likely to start a fight.
example_usage: Directed at someone who's done something truly contemptible: betrayal, theft, serious disrespect. Also used in road rage. Never, ever said around elders or in mixed company.

regional_variations.note: Can provoke physical violence; deeply offensive
Still very strong but somewhat normalized through overuse

meaning: Combining 'mādar' (mother) with 'jendeh' (whore) to create one of Persian's most devastating compound insults. It attacks the mother's sexual honor directly, which in Iranian culture is effectively attacking the entire family's reputation. The insult isn't about the target — it's about destroying their family's dignity.
cultural_context: The mother-honor system in Iranian culture means maternal insults carry weight that's difficult to convey in English. 'Mādar jendeh' doesn't just insult a person; it retroactively shames their entire lineage. Even Iranians who've lived abroad for decades report that hearing this triggers a visceral reaction that purely English insults don't.
example_usage: Reserved for genuine rage or deep personal enmity. This is a bridge-burning insult — relationships don't recover from it. Also appears in heated political discourse about despised figures.

meaning: The standard vulgar Persian verb for sexual intercourse. Unlike English 'fuck,' which has migrated into a thousand non-sexual uses, 'gāyidan' retains its sexual punch more consistently. However, it does appear in figurative uses: 'gāyid' (he fucked it up) and the ubiquitous 'gāyidamesh' (I fucked it/I ruined it).
cultural_context: This is the verb root behind 'mādar-e-to gāyidam' — the most infamous Persian insult. On its own, it's simply 'to fuck,' but in Iranian cultural context where sexual topics are deeply taboo in public discourse, even the verb form carries more shock than English 'fuck.' The gap between ta'arof (formal politeness) and this word is enormous.
example_usage: Figurative: 'Gāyidamesh' — I fucked it up / I ruined it. Used among male friends. The literal sexual use is confined to very private contexts.

meaning: The vulgar Persian word for vagina — the root of an enormous profanity family. 'Kos' by itself is crude but it's the derivatives that make it devastating: koskesh (pimp), kos-e naneh (mother's cunt), kos nagu (don't talk shit), kos khol (vagina-brained/idiot). Understanding 'kos' unlocks half of Persian profanity.
cultural_context: In a culture where female sexuality is tightly controlled and rarely discussed publicly, 'kos' carries extraordinary taboo weight. It's not just a body part — it's a violation of the entire modesty system. The word appears in pre-Islamic Persian poetry (Rumi's less-quoted works include it), proving that Persians have always had a complicated relationship with prudishness.
example_usage: Rarely used standalone — almost always in compounds. 'Kos' is the building block, not the finished product. When used alone, it's either a crude anatomical reference or an exclamation of shock.

meaning: Literally 'kos' (vagina) + 'khol' (related to emptiness/foolishness) — someone whose brain has been replaced by a vagina. It means profoundly stupid, scatter-brained, idiotic. It's one of Persian's most commonly used insults because it perfectly captures the concept of someone too dumb to function.
cultural_context: The misogynistic etymology is obvious — equating femaleness with stupidity — but modern usage has largely detached from gender. Men are called 'koskhol' far more often than women. Among younger Tehranis and diaspora Persians, it's become a casual insult equivalent to 'dumbass,' used between friends without much heat. The gender critique is starting to surface in Iranian feminist discourse.
example_usage: Between friends: 'Koskhol, chi kar kardi?' — dumbass, what did you do? Applied to men and women alike for acts of obvious stupidity. Very common in casual speech.

regional_variations.note: Extremely common; used as casually as 'dumbass'
The 'kos' root retains full taboo weight

literal_translation: dull, slow (originally: a type of dull horse)
meaning: Originally describing a dull, sluggish horse that doesn't respond to commands — now applied to people who are slow to understand, absent-minded, or just not very bright. It's one of Persian's gentler insults for stupidity, the kind grandmothers use without feeling guilty.
cultural_context: The equestrian origin reflects Iran's deep horse culture — Persian has many animal-derived insults, and horse-related ones specifically target intelligence and responsiveness. 'Kheng' is mild enough for family television. It sits at the bottom of Persian's stupidity-insult spectrum, below 'ahmagh,' 'oskol,' and far below 'koskhol.'
example_usage: Said with exasperation to someone who's not getting it: 'Kheng-i mage? Do bar goftam!' — are you thick? I said it twice! Gentle enough for use with children.

meaning: 'Nāmus' is one of Persian's most loaded concepts — it means honor, specifically family honor tied to female relatives' chastity and reputation. 'Bi nāmus' (without nāmus) is therefore one of the worst things you can call someone: a man who has failed to protect his family's honor, or worse, doesn't care about it.
cultural_context: The nāmus system underpins much of Iranian social structure. A man's worth is partially measured by his ability to protect the honor (read: sexual reputation) of his female relatives. Calling someone 'bi nāmus' attacks the foundation of their social identity. In traditional communities, this insult can trigger honor-based violence. Among westernized/diaspora Iranians, it's still deeply offensive but more likely to prompt argument than fists.
example_usage: Directed at someone who's done something deeply unethical, especially related to betraying trust or disrespecting family. 'Bi nāmus' is character assassination in two words.

regional_variations.note: Can provoke extreme reactions; touches deepest values
Still strong but less likely to trigger violence

meaning: 'Gheirat' is the emotional component of honor — the protective jealousy a man is expected to feel about his family, particularly female relatives. 'Bi gheirat' means lacking this quality: a man who doesn't defend his family, doesn't react to disrespect, doesn't care. It questions someone's fundamental masculinity and moral character.
cultural_context: If 'nāmus' is the concept and 'gheirat' is the feeling, then 'bi gheirat' attacks the emotional engine that drives the honor system. It's a uniquely Persian/Middle Eastern insult that has no real English equivalent — 'coward' is too narrow, 'dishonorable' too formal. Modern Iranian feminists have critiqued 'gheirat' as patriarchal possessiveness, creating generational tension around this word.
example_usage: Deployed when a man fails to stand up for his family or partner. 'Bi gheirat, zant-o doost-esh dāre mizane va to hich nemikonī' — you spineless coward, his friend hits your wife and you do nothing.

meaning: Literally 'without sharaf' (nobility/honor). While similar to 'bi nāmus,' this targets general moral character rather than specifically family-sexual honor. A 'bi sharaf' person is a liar, a cheat, someone who breaks promises — dishonorable in the broader sense. It's a serious insult but less explosive than 'bi nāmus.'
cultural_context: 'Sharaf' comes from Arabic and connects to a broader Islamic concept of noble character. The insult works in any Persian-speaking context because it attacks universal moral failure rather than the specifically Iranian nāmus system. It's commonly used in political discourse — corrupt politicians are frequently called 'bi sharaf.'
example_usage: Said about someone who's betrayed trust or acted dishonestly: 'In ādam bi sharaf-e' — this person is dishonorable. Common in political rants and personal betrayal situations.

meaning: 'Nā' (not) + 'mard' (man) — not a man, someone who fails to meet the standards of manhood. This doesn't mean effeminate; it means untrustworthy, cowardly, and disloyal. A 'nāmard' breaks promises, abandons friends in trouble, and stabs people in the back. It's a moral judgment wrapped in a gender expectation.
cultural_context: Persian poetry and literature are saturated with the 'mard' (man) ideal — bravery, loyalty, keeping one's word. Ferdowsi's Shahnameh established 'mardānegi' (manliness) as Iran's foundational virtue. 'Nāmard' attacks someone by measuring them against 2,500 years of heroic tradition and finding them wanting. It stings because it carries epic weight.
example_usage: 'Nāmard, goftī komak mikonī!' — you coward/backstabber, you said you'd help! Used when someone fails to keep a promise or abandons you when you need them.

meaning: The straightforward Persian word for coward — someone who's afraid. Unlike 'nāmard' (which carries moral weight) or 'bi gheirat' (which carries honor-system implications), 'tarsu' is simple: you're scared. It's the insult children use on the playground and adults use when they want to goad someone into action.
cultural_context: Mild enough for all contexts. Iranian children use it constantly — daring friends to jump off walls, approach crushes, or eat strange food. As an adult insult it's more teasing than aggressive. In a culture with heavier words for cowardice ('nāmard,' 'bi gheirat'), 'tarsu' feels almost affectionate.
example_usage: Playground goading: 'Tarsu! Nemituni bepri!' — scaredy-cat! You can't jump! Also used between adults as light teasing.

meaning: Having the heart of a goat — timid, fearful, easily startled. 'Bozdel' is more literary and formal than 'tarsu' but covers the same territory. The goat metaphor is vivid: goats are nervous animals that bolt at the slightest disturbance, and a 'bozdel' person does the same.
cultural_context: The goat-heart image connects to Iran's pastoral heritage where animal behavior was closely observed and metaphorically applied to humans. In Shahnameh-era Persian, 'bozdel' was a serious accusation against warriors. In modern usage it's softened into a standard insult for cowardice, more formal than 'tarsu' but less loaded than 'nāmard.'
example_usage: 'Adam-e bozdeli hasti' — you're a cowardly person. Used in arguments when someone refuses to take a stand or avoid confrontation.

meaning: Another term for pimp — 'jā' (place) + 'kesh' (puller), one who arranges places for sexual encounters. Functionally identical to 'koskesh' as a nuclear insult, though some speakers consider 'jākesh' slightly less crude because it avoids the anatomical 'kos.' This distinction is academic when someone's screaming it at you in traffic.
cultural_context: Like 'koskesh,' this insult strikes at the deepest vulnerability in the Iranian honor system: the implication that a man profits from female sexual activity, implicitly involving his own family. The word exists in Turkish too ('pezevenk' fills the same niche), reflecting shared Persianate culture across the region.
example_usage: Same explosive contexts as 'koskesh' — extreme anger, road rage, genuine enmity. The kind of word that makes bystanders look away nervously.

meaning: The standard vulgar word for buttocks/anus. It's the root of 'kuni' (gay, already in the database) and dozens of compound expressions. On its own it's crude but not devastating — more like English 'ass' than anything nuclear. 'Kun' generates much of Persian's anal-themed insult vocabulary.
cultural_context: The anus in Persian profanity serves as both a sexual and a degradation reference. 'Kun' compounds work differently from 'kos' compounds: where 'kos' attacks family honor, 'kun' attacks personal dignity and masculinity. 'Kun dādan' (to give ass) means to submit/be passive, connecting physical and social submission.
example_usage: In compounds: 'kun-e goshād' (wide-assed, meaning shameless), 'kun-esh mikhareh' (his ass itches, meaning he's restless/looking for trouble). Rarely used standalone.

meaning: Having a 'wide ass' — meaning someone who is shameless, unbothered by criticism, and immune to embarrassment. The logic (vulgar but internally consistent): someone whose ass is 'wide' from overuse has lost all dignity. It's applied to shameless people regardless of sexuality — it's about brazenness, not anatomy.
cultural_context: In a culture where shame ('sharm') and modesty ('hayā') are central social values, calling someone 'kun-e goshād' says they've discarded the very mechanisms that make someone a functioning member of society. It's particularly devastating because Iranian culture polices shame carefully — to be called shameless is to be called not fully human.
example_usage: About someone who lies shamelessly, takes credit for others' work, or behaves outrageously without remorse: 'In ādam kheili kun-e goshād-e' — this person is completely shameless.

meaning: Lacking 'hayā' — the Persian concept of modesty, shame, and appropriate self-restraint. It's the clean version of 'kun-e goshād,' conveying the same essential criticism (you have no shame) without the anal imagery. Acceptable in most social settings while still being a genuine insult.
cultural_context: 'Hayā' in Persian culture is a virtue so fundamental that its absence defines a character flaw. It's close to Japanese 'haji' (shame) in its social function — without it, a person is socially adrift. 'Bi hayā' is commonly used by older Iranians about younger generations' behavior, creating a reliable generational conflict flashpoint.
example_usage: A grandmother about her granddaughter's Instagram: 'Bi hayā, in chieh gozāshti?' — shameless girl, what's this you've posted? Also used seriously about genuinely brazen behavior.

meaning: Lacking 'adab' — manners, politeness, and proper social behavior. In a culture built on ta'arof (elaborate ritualized politeness), being 'bi adab' is a significant social failing. It's mild as insults go but potent in context: you're saying someone has failed at the most basic requirement of Persian social life.
cultural_context: Ta'arof — the Persian system of ritual politeness — is so central to Iranian identity that failing at it ('bi adab') is a genuine character criticism. A 'bi adab' person doesn't rise when elders enter, doesn't perform the expected verbal courtesies, doesn't offer tea. In a culture where form matters as much as content, this is not a minor complaint.
example_usage: Said about someone who doesn't greet properly, interrupts elders, or skips social niceties: 'Che bache-ye bi adabi!' — what a rude child! Common parental reprimand.

meaning: Someone who wasn't raised properly — lacking 'tarbiat' (upbringing, discipline, education). This insult is clever because it attacks the parents as much as the target: if you're 'bi tarbiat,' your family failed to civilize you. It's a two-for-one insult.
cultural_context: In Iranian family culture, a child's behavior reflects directly on the parents. Calling someone 'bi tarbiat' is saying their entire family failed at the most basic task of raising a functional human. This indirect family insult makes it sting more than it seems — you're not just rude, your whole family produced rudeness.
example_usage: 'In bache kheili bi tarbiat-e, mādaresh chikar mikoneh?' — this child is so badly raised, what is his mother doing? The mother-blaming is the quiet part said loud.

meaning: From 'lāsh' (carcass/corpse), calling someone 'lāshi' means they're morally dead — a worthless, contemptible lowlife. It implies someone who does disgusting things without conscience. In Tehran street slang, 'lāshi' has become one of the most common character insults, covering everyone from petty cheaters to serious criminals.
cultural_context: Originally underworld slang that migrated into mainstream youth vocabulary through Iranian cinema and street culture. Tehran's 'lāt-o-lut' (thug) culture, which has its own elaborate honor code and vocabulary, contributed 'lāshi' to the general lexicon. In that world, being called 'lāshi' by another street-level person is a serious provocation.
example_usage: 'Ādam-e lāshi-ye, behesh e'temād nakon' — he's a scumbag, don't trust him. Used about someone who's proven themselves unreliable or immoral.

regional_variations.note: Core vocabulary of south Tehran slang
Milder when used outside street context

meaning: Calling someone literal garbage. It's straightforward and effective — no metaphor to decode, no cultural system to understand. You're trash. Used for people, their work, their ideas, their possessions. 'Āshghāl' is the universal Persian expression of contempt for quality.
cultural_context: While many Persian insults require cultural context to land (the honor system, the nāmus concept), 'āshghāl' is blissfully direct. It works across all Persian-speaking contexts — Iran, Afghanistan, Tajikistan, diaspora. Its simplicity is its strength. Even non-Persian speakers in those regions understand it.
example_usage: 'In ghazā āshghāl-e' — this food is garbage. 'Āshghāl!' — directed at a person who's done something contemptible. Versatile across all registers except formal.

meaning: From 'avaz' (exchange/substitute), implying someone who shouldn't exist — a 'replacement' child, a mistake. In modern usage it's simply 'bastard' or 'asshole' without the literal illegitimacy implication. It's one of Persian's most versatile general-purpose insults for someone you despise.
cultural_context: The word's evolution from 'substituted one' to 'bastard' suggests an old euphemism for illegitimacy that lost its euphemistic quality. In modern Tehran slang, 'avazi' is common and harsh but not nuclear — it's the word you reach for when someone cuts in line, steals your taxi, or does something selfish. Below 'koskesh' but above 'ahmagh.'
example_usage: 'In ādam-e avazi chera in kār-o kard?' — why did this bastard do this? Common in everyday frustration, road rage, and workplace complaints.

meaning: Low, base, despicable — someone of the lowest moral character. 'Adam-e past-i' means 'you're a lowlife.' It's a clean insult that can be used in formal settings while still carrying real contempt. The word works as both an adjective and, implicitly, a noun.
cultural_context: Persian's social hierarchy vocabulary runs deep — centuries of courtly culture created a rich language for ranking people's character. 'Past' sits at the bottom of this moral hierarchy. It's one of those insults that's more devastating in formal contexts because it sounds like a considered judgment rather than emotional outburst.
example_usage: 'Adam-e past-o-i' — you're a despicable person. 'Kār-e past-i kard' — he did a despicable thing. Usable in formal complaints, legal contexts, newspaper editorials.

meaning: A literary-register insult meaning vile and contemptible. It comes from Arabic and carries a formal weight that street slang lacks. Calling someone 'rezel' is like delivering a verdict — it's measured, deliberate, and final. It implies the speaker has thought carefully and concluded you're irredeemable.
cultural_context: Persian's dual vocabulary — native Persian words alongside Arabic borrowings — means every concept has two registers. 'Past' (Persian, everyday) and 'rezel' (Arabic, literary) mean roughly the same thing, but 'rezel' sounds like it's been written in a legal document or spoken from a pulpit. It's the insult of educated contempt.
example_usage: 'Ādam-e rezli hasti' — you're a vile person. Used in writing more than speech; when spoken, it signals educated anger rather than street rage.

meaning: Calling someone a donkey — stubborn and stupid. Similar to 'khar' (already in the database) but more formal. Where 'khar' is the everyday word, 'olāgh' is its slightly more literary cousin. Both mean the same thing: you have the intelligence of a pack animal.
cultural_context: Persian has two words for donkey, and both are insults. 'Khar' is direct and common; 'olāgh' has a more rural, earthy quality. In some dialects, 'olāgh' is actually harsher than 'khar' because it sounds more deliberate — 'khar' can slip out casually, but 'olāgh' sounds like you mean it.
example_usage: 'Olāgh, nemifāhmi?' — jackass, don't you understand? Used when someone is being stubbornly stupid.

meaning: Calling someone a dog — in Persian/Islamic culture, one of the most degrading animal comparisons possible. Dogs in traditional Iranian culture are considered najis (ritually unclean), making 'sag' an insult that attacks both character and spiritual status. It implies someone is dirty, low, and unworthy of respect.
cultural_context: The gulf between Western and Iranian attitudes toward dogs makes this insult culturally fascinating. In English, 'dog' as an insult is almost playful; in Persian, it draws on centuries of Islamic teaching about dogs' ritual impurity. 'Pedar sag' (dog father, already in the database) is the compound form. However, younger, westernized Iranians increasingly keep dogs as pets, creating tension around the word's power.
example_usage: 'Mesle sag' — like a dog (degraded, subservient). 'Sag-e man bood' — he was my dog (my servant/lackey). The word carries more weight in traditional contexts.

regional_variations.note: Dogs remain deeply najis; the insult lands hard
Dog ownership is growing; the insult is softening

english_equivalent: son of a bitch, little shit
meaning: A puppy — but in Persian, this isn't cute. Calling someone a dog's offspring combines the insult of 'sag' (dog) with the diminution of being a baby animal. It's worse than 'sag' alone because it adds contempt through smallness: you're not even a full dog, you're its worthless offspring.
cultural_context: The compound structure mirrors 'pedar sag' but works in the other direction — instead of 'your father is a dog,' it's 'you are a dog's puppy.' Both attack lineage, which is the core mechanism of Persian insult culture. The word is common in heated arguments and street confrontations.
example_usage: Directed at someone younger or lower-status who's being disrespectful: 'Tule sag, to ki hasti ke bā man harf mizani?' — you little shit, who are you to talk to me?

literal_translation: damn dog / dog with a cause
english_equivalent: son of a gun, damn bastard (grudging admiration)
meaning: A strange insult that often conveys grudging admiration rather than contempt. 'Sag-e masab' is what you call someone who's cleverly outsmarted you, pulled off something impressive, or done something you secretly admire. It's 'you magnificent bastard' in Persian — the dog insult softened by 'masab' into something almost complimentary.
cultural_context: This is one of Persian's best examples of an insult functioning as a compliment. In Iranian 'ta'arof' culture, where direct praise can be seen as suspicious, expressing admiration through an insult is actually more genuine. 'Sag-e masab, chi kār kardi!' (you dog, what did you do!) said with a smile is high praise.
example_usage: With grudging admiration: 'In sag-e masab che gol-i zad!' — this bastard scored an incredible goal! With genuine anger: context and tone make it a real insult.

meaning: A derogatory diminutive of 'mard' (man) — calling someone a 'little man' to belittle them. It's used by women about men they despise, by elders about younger men who've overstepped, and by anyone who wants to reduce a man to insignificance. The '-ikeh' suffix drips with contempt.
cultural_context: In a culture where being a 'mard' (man) carries enormous weight (bravery, honor, reliability), being called 'martikeh' — a miniature, fake, lesser version of a man — is devastating. It's particularly effective when used by women, because it simultaneously uses and subverts the masculine honor system.
example_usage: A wife about her ex-husband: 'In martikeh fekr mikone ki-e?' — this little man thinks he's somebody? Common in domestic disputes and gossip.

meaning: The female equivalent of 'martikeh' — a derogatory diminutive of 'zan' (woman). It reduces a woman to something small, contemptible, and unworthy of the dignity the word 'zan' normally carries. Used by men and women alike to express contempt for a specific woman.
cultural_context: The parallel construction of 'martikeh' (diminished man) and 'zanikeh' (diminished woman) shows how Persian creates gendered insults by shrinking people below their expected status. 'Zanikeh' is used in gossip, domestic conflicts, and whenever someone wants to delegitimize a woman without using overtly sexual insults.
example_usage: 'In zanikeh che goft?' — what did that woman say? (dripping with contempt). Used when referring to a woman you don't respect.

meaning: A woman of loose morals — promiscuous, shameless, without sexual restraint. While 'jendeh' is the blunt word for 'whore,' 'harzeh' is more about character than profession. A 'harzeh' isn't necessarily a sex worker; she's a woman who violates the modesty code. The distinction matters in a culture where the code is everything.
cultural_context: In Iran's gender-policed society, 'harzeh' is one of the most damaging labels a woman can receive. It doesn't require proof — the accusation itself causes social damage. The word's power comes from the 'hayā' (modesty) system: to be 'harzeh' is to have abandoned the defining female virtue. Iranian feminists have fought against this word's social power for decades.
example_usage: Used as gossip that destroys reputations: 'Migi harzeh-ast?' — you're saying she's loose? The word functions as social weapon, not just insult.

meaning: The formal/literary word for prostitute, from Arabic. Where 'jendeh' is street-level vulgarity, 'fāhesheh' is the word used in legal documents, religious texts, and news reports. As an insult, it's deliberately cold and clinical — calling someone a 'fāhesheh' instead of 'jendeh' sounds like a legal sentence rather than a heated outburst.
cultural_context: The Arabic/formal register gives 'fāhesheh' a quality of objective judgment that 'jendeh' lacks. It appears in Quranic commentary and Iranian law. Using it as an insult implies you've considered the evidence and reached a verdict. This deliberateness makes it differently hurtful than 'jendeh' — less hot anger, more cold contempt.
example_usage: In formal or quasi-legal contexts: 'Zan-e fāhesheh' — a prostitute woman. Used when the speaker wants to sound authoritative rather than emotional.

meaning: The native Persian formal word for prostitute — less common than 'jendeh' (street) or 'fāhesheh' (Arabic/legal) but existing as a literary option. 'Ruspi' has an archaic quality that makes it sound almost poetic, like English 'harlot.' It's the word you'd encounter in classical Persian literature rather than on Tehran streets.
cultural_context: Persian's triple vocabulary for prostitution — ruspi (native formal), fāhesheh (Arabic formal), jendeh (vulgar) — reflects how central sexual honor is to the culture. Every register needs its own word because the concept is discussed in every context from poetry to law to street fights. The word's rarity in modern speech makes it feel deliberately literary when used.
example_usage: Primarily literary/historical. In modern speech, using 'ruspi' instead of 'jendeh' sounds intentionally archaic — like calling someone a 'harlot' in English.

meaning: Another nuclear maternal insult using 'ghahbeh' (an older/literary word for prostitute). It functions identically to 'mādar jendeh' but with a slightly different register — 'ghahbeh' is less street-level than 'jendeh,' giving this insult an almost formal quality of degradation. The effect is the same: total destruction of family honor.
cultural_context: 'Ghahbeh' is interesting because it comes from Arabic 'qahba' and sounds more 'educated' than 'jendeh.' Some speakers feel 'mādar ghahbeh' is actually worse than 'mādar jendeh' because the formal register makes it sound premeditated rather than heat-of-the-moment. Deliberate dishonor stings more than impulsive rage.
example_usage: Same nuclear contexts as 'mādar jendeh' — genuine rage, extreme provocation. The formality makes it sound colder and more deliberate.

meaning: The standard vulgar Persian word for penis. Like 'kos' (vagina), it's a root word that generates an entire profanity family: 'be kiram' (on my dick = I don't care), 'kiri' (dickish = shitty), 'kir-e khar' (donkey's dick). On its own it's simply a crude anatomical term, but its derivatives power much of Persian vulgarity.
cultural_context: In public discourse in Iran, this word is completely taboo — you won't hear it on television, in newspapers, or in mixed-gender polite company. But among male friends (and increasingly in mixed groups among younger Iranians), 'kir' and its derivatives flow freely. The gap between public and private Persian is one of the language's defining features.
example_usage: Mostly in compounds: 'be kiram' (I don't give a shit), 'kir-e khar' (insult). Standalone use is crude anatomical reference or exclamation of frustration.

meaning: Combining two of Persian profanity's most productive roots — 'kir' (dick) and 'khar' (donkey) — into one compound insult. Someone with the intelligence of a donkey's penis. The image is deliberately absurd and degrading: you're not even a whole donkey, you're the stupidest part of the stupidest animal.
cultural_context: The donkey occupies a special place in Persian insult culture as the embodiment of stupidity. Its penis is therefore the stupidest part of stupidity incarnate — an insult within an insult. The compound is common in street language and among younger men, but would be deeply shocking in any formal or family context.
example_usage: 'Kir-e khar, in che kāri-e kardi?' — you dipshit, what have you done? Said in moments of exasperation at someone's stupidity.

english_equivalent: to screw up, to mess up, to make a mistake
meaning: Literally 'to eat shit,' but in practice it means 'to screw up' or 'to do something you'll regret.' 'Goh khordam' (I ate shit) is a common way of saying 'I fucked up.' It can also be a threat: 'goh khordi' (you ate shit = you'll regret what you just did/said).
cultural_context: The 'goh' (shit) family is one of Persian profanity's most productive branches. 'Goh khordan' extends beyond simple scatology into a complete system: 'goh khordi' as a warning, 'goh khordam' as regret, 'goh zadan' (to shit-hit = to ruin), 'goh-e ziādi khordan' (to eat too much shit = to overstep). Shit-eating in Persian is an entire philosophical framework.
example_usage: Regret: 'Goh khordam ke raft-am' — I fucked up by going. Warning: 'Goh khordi, agar dobare in kār-o bokoni' — you'll be sorry if you do that again.

literal_translation: to shit-hit / to ruin with shit
english_equivalent: to mess up, to ruin, to fuck up
meaning: To ruin something — literally to hit it with shit. 'Goh zad be hame chiz' means 'he ruined everything.' Where 'goh khordan' (eating shit) is about personal mistakes, 'goh zadan' (hitting with shit) is about ruining things for others. The shit is projectile here, not consumed.
cultural_context: Part of the complete 'goh' system that makes Persian scatological profanity remarkably precise. You eat shit (personal mistake), you hit with shit (ruin things), you step in shit (get into trouble), you are shit (worthless). Each preposition and verb creates a distinct meaning from the same root material.
example_usage: 'Goh zad be kār-emun' — he fucked up our work/plan. 'Goh zadi behesh' — you ruined it. Said in exasperation when someone's actions have destroyed a plan or situation.

english_equivalent: to shit, to crap, to screw up
meaning: The vulgar verb for defecation, also used figuratively for 'to completely fail' or 'to ruin.' 'Ridam behesh' (I shat on it) means 'I don't give a shit about it.' 'Ridi' (you shat) means 'you screwed up.' More aggressive than 'goh khordan' — this is shitting on something, not accidentally eating it.
cultural_context: While 'goh' words tend to be about mess and mistake, 'ridan' carries active contempt or deliberate failure. 'Ridam be in zendegi' (I shit on this life) is a common expression of existential frustration. The distinction between 'goh khordan' (I messed up, my fault) and 'ridan' (I actively don't care, everything is shit) reveals a philosophical divide in Persian scatology.
example_usage: Contempt: 'Ridam be hame chiz' — I shit on everything (nothing matters). Failure: 'Ridi, dāsh!' — you screwed up, bro!

meaning: The vulgar verb for urination. Figuratively, 'shāshidam be X' (I pissed on X) means extreme contempt or disrespect. Less harsh than 'ridan' (shitting) but in the same contempt family. 'Shāsh' (piss) as a noun also means something worthless.
cultural_context: Persian's urination vocabulary is less developed than its defecation vocabulary — 'goh' generates far more idioms than 'shāsh.' But 'shāshidan' fills an important middle ground between mild and severe contempt. 'Shāshidan be X' is how Iranians express supreme indifference — you're not even worth proper anger.
example_usage: 'Shāshidam be in ghānun' — I piss on this law (extreme contempt for a rule). 'Shāsh-e khod-et' — it's your own piss (your own problem/mess).

meaning: The aggressive Persian 'shut up' — literally 'suffocate yourself.' It's harsher than 'sāket bāsh' (be quiet) because the image is violent: stop talking by choking. It's the standard way to aggressively silence someone in an argument without resorting to sexual or family insults.
cultural_context: Persian has a politeness spectrum for 'shut up': 'lotfan sāket bāsh' (please be quiet) → 'sāket bāsh' (be quiet) → 'dahan-et-o beband' (close your mouth) → 'khafeh sho' (suffocate) → 'goh bokhor' (eat shit). 'Khafeh sho' sits at the aggressive-but-not-vulgar sweet spot — rude but not obscene.
example_usage: 'Khafeh sho, dāri gooh migi!' — shut up, you're talking shit! Used in heated arguments, between siblings, and in traffic.

meaning: A direct command to close your mouth — slightly less violent than 'khafeh sho' (suffocate) but more aggressive than 'sāket bāsh' (be quiet). It's the middle rung on Persian's silence-command ladder. The 'ro' (را) makes it specifically about the mouth as an object to be physically closed.
cultural_context: In ta'arof culture, even telling someone to be quiet requires calibration. Persian speakers instinctively choose from five or six levels of 'shut up' depending on their relationship with the listener, the social setting, and how angry they are. 'Dahan-et ro beband' works between friends or in moderate anger.
example_usage: 'Dahan-et ro beband, hameh dāran gush midan!' — shut your mouth, everyone's listening! Said in situations where someone is embarrassing themselves or others.

meaning: The standard aggressive Persian dismissal — 'gom' (lost) + 'sho' (become). Get lost. Disappear. It's direct and hostile without being vulgar, making it versatile across most social contexts. 'Gomsho' is the Persian equivalent of English 'get out of here' — forceful but clean.
cultural_context: Part of a dismissal spectrum: 'boro' (go) → 'gomsho' (get lost) → 'boroo gomsho' (go get lost, emphatic) → 'gom-o-gur sho' (get lost and buried). Each level adds aggression. 'Gomsho' is the default aggressive dismissal — the one that comes out first when you want someone gone.
example_usage: 'Gomsho az jolo-ye cheshm-am!' — get out of my sight! 'Gomsho, nemikham bebinam-et' — get lost, I don't want to see you.

english_equivalent: fuck off and die, disappear completely
meaning: The escalated version of 'gomsho' — not just get lost, but get lost AND buried. 'Gur' means grave, so you're telling someone to disappear into a grave. It's a dramatic Iranian way of saying 'I want you completely erased from existence.' The death imagery elevates a simple dismissal into something theatrical and absolute.
cultural_context: Persian love of dramatic expression meets death imagery. 'Gom-o-gur sho' is the kind of phrase that sounds terrifying in translation but in practice is used in arguments between family members who'll be having tea together an hour later. Iranian emotional expression runs hot — words of death and graves are deployed freely and rarely meant literally.
example_usage: 'Gom-o-gur sho, digeh nemikhām bebinam-et!' — disappear completely, I never want to see you again! (May actually see them at dinner.)

meaning: 'Go die' — two words, maximum dismissal. It sounds extreme in English but in Persian emotional register it's surprisingly common. It's the ultimate expression of 'I'm done with you.' Between siblings it can be almost playful; between enemies it's dead serious. Context is everything.
cultural_context: Iranian emotional expression doesn't have the same restraint around death language that English does. 'Boro bemir' is said in the same spirit that Americans say 'go to hell' — shocking if translated literally but calibrated to its own cultural norms. Mothers have said it to disobedient teenagers and then made them lunch. The gap between word and intent is distinctly Iranian.
example_usage: Between siblings: 'Boro bemir!' (drop dead! = leave me alone). In genuine anger: 'Boro bemir, ghiyāfe-at-o nemikhām bebinam' — go die, I don't want to see your face.

meaning: Invoking the person who washes corpses for burial. 'Mordeh shur-et bebareh' means 'may the corpse washer take you' — a poetic way of saying 'may you die.' The profession of washing the dead is considered both sacred and deeply ominous in Iranian culture, making this invocation eerily specific.
cultural_context: In Islamic burial tradition, the corpse must be ritually washed (ghusl) before burial. The person who does this — the mordeh shur — occupies a strange cultural position: necessary but feared, respected but avoided. Invoking them is like invoking death itself through its most hands-on representative. The phrase has a gothic quality that makes it uniquely Persian.
example_usage: 'Mordeh shur-et bebareh!' — may the corpse washer take you! Said in exasperation, not genuine death wish. 'Mordeh shur-e in zendegi ro bebareh' — damn this life.

meaning: Someone so pathetic they're like a humiliated corpse — not just dead but dishonored in death. It's used to describe someone who's completely beaten down, useless, or pitifully submissive. The compound creates an image of degradation that goes beyond life into death.
cultural_context: Persian death-related insults form their own subcategory. 'Zalil mordeh' is used particularly about people in positions of weakness or submission — a man who lets his boss humiliate him, someone who accepts mistreatment without fighting back. In Iran's hierarchical social structure, being 'zalil mordeh' means you've accepted your degradation.
example_usage: 'In adam-e zalil mordeh hich vaght az khod-esh defā' nemikone' — this pathetic wretch never defends himself. Used about people perceived as spineless.

meaning: Literally 'bad fortune/luck' — used as both a genuine expression of pity and a cutting insult depending on tone. As pity: 'badbakht, che bala-yi sar-esh umad' (poor wretch, what happened to him). As insult: 'in badbakht nemiduneh chi kār koneh' (this loser doesn't know what to do). The word's duality makes it uniquely flexible.
cultural_context: Persian's fatalistic streak — the idea that destiny (bakht/taqdeer) shapes life — infuses 'badbakht' with philosophical weight. You're not just calling someone a loser; you're declaring their entire fate cursed. In a culture that takes destiny seriously, this isn't just an insult, it's a cosmological judgment. It can be devastatingly sympathetic or devastatingly cruel.
example_usage: Pity: 'Badbakht, pedar-esh mordeh' — poor wretch, his father died. Insult: 'Badbakht, hichi balad nist' — pathetic loser, he's good at nothing.

meaning: 'Without a solution' — someone trapped by circumstance with no way out. Unlike 'badbakht' (bad fate), 'bichāreh' is almost always sympathetic. It's what Iranians say about people they pity. As a mild insult, it implies helpless incompetence: 'the poor fool doesn't even know how bad things are.'
cultural_context: More pity than insult in most uses. Iranian culture has a strong sympathy-expression system, and 'bichāreh' is its anchor word. 'Vāy, bichāreh!' (oh, poor thing!) is the reflexive Iranian response to anyone's misfortune. When used as an insult, the pity itself is the blade — being pitied in a culture that values strength is its own degradation.
example_usage: Sympathetic: 'Bichāreh, kār-esh ro az dast dādeh' — poor thing, he lost his job. Condescending: 'Bichāreh, fekr mikoneh kasi behesh sar mizaneh' — poor thing, he thinks someone will visit him.

meaning: Calling someone a jackal — a scavenger animal that feeds on others' kills and lurks in the shadows. In Persian, 'chāghāl' implies someone who's sneaky, untrustworthy, and profits from others' misfortune. It's become extremely popular in modern Tehran street slang, especially among younger speakers.
cultural_context: The jackal in Persian culture is the anti-lion: where the lion (shir) represents courage and nobility (Iran's national symbol), the jackal represents cowardice and parasitism. Calling someone 'chāghāl' places them at the bottom of the animal-honor hierarchy. In modern youth slang, it's evolved into a more general 'lowlife/scumbag' insult divorced from its zoological origins.
example_usage: 'In chāghāl-ā hameye pool-o khordand' — these jackals took all the money. 'Chāghāl' as a standalone insult in confrontations.

regional_variations.note: Very common among youth; can provoke fights

meaning: Calling someone a pig — in Islamic culture, the most ritually unclean animal. While dogs are najis, pigs are haram on another level: you can't touch them, eat them, or even keep them. Calling someone 'khuk' attacks their cleanliness, morality, and spiritual status simultaneously.
cultural_context: The Islamic prohibition on pork gives 'khuk' a religious dimension absent in Western pig-insults. In English, calling someone a pig implies messiness or greed. In Persian, it implies spiritual contamination — you're not just dirty, you're forbidden. This makes 'khuk' heavier than its English equivalent, particularly for religious Iranians.
example_usage: 'Mesle khuk mizaneh' — he eats like a pig. 'Khuk' directed at a person for disgusting behavior, greed, or moral filth.

meaning: Calling someone an 'animal' — implying they lack the rationality and civility that separates humans from beasts. Unlike specific animal insults (khar, sag, khuk), 'heyvān' is the generic: you're not even worth specifying which animal you resemble. It implies brutish, uncivilized behavior across the board.
cultural_context: Persian philosophy, influenced by both Zoroastrian and Islamic thought, places humans distinctly above animals through the capacity for reason ('aghl'). Calling someone 'heyvān' says they've forfeited this divine gift. It's used for violent behavior, sexual impropriety, and general loss of self-control — anything that makes someone seem more beast than human.
example_usage: 'Heyvān, dast nazan behesh!' — animal, don't touch her! Used when someone's behavior becomes threatening or brutish.

meaning: Wild, uncivilized, savage — someone who behaves without regard for social norms or human decency. It's a step beyond 'heyvān' (animal): a 'heyvān' is merely brutish, but a 'vahshi' is dangerously uncontrolled. The word carries echoes of the civilized/barbarian divide that's deep in Persian cultural DNA.
cultural_context: Iran's identity as a civilization (as opposed to merely a country) makes 'vahshi' particularly loaded. Persia defined itself against 'barbarism' for millennia — calling someone 'vahshi' places them outside 2,500 years of civilizational achievement. It's used for violent behavior but also for cultural crudeness, like someone who doesn't know how to eat properly at a dinner party.
example_usage: 'Vahshi bāzi dar nayār!' — don't act like a savage! 'Mesle vahshi-hā raftār mikoneh' — he behaves like a barbarian. Applied to both physical violence and social gracelessness.

meaning: Lacking 'farhang' — culture, education, refinement. In a civilization that invented the word 'paradise' and gave the world Rumi and Hafez, calling someone 'bi farhang' is saying they've failed to participate in humanity's highest achievements. It attacks someone's intellectual and aesthetic capacities simultaneously.
cultural_context: Iranians take enormous pride in their cultural heritage — 'farhang' isn't just culture, it's civilization itself. 'Bi farhang' is used particularly about diaspora Iranians who've 'lost' their culture, about people who are rich but tasteless, and about anyone who demonstrates that money and breeding are different things. It's the educated Iranian's favorite put-down.
example_usage: 'Ādam-e bi farhang-i, az honar chizi nemifahmi' — you're an uncultured person, you don't understand anything about art. The ultimate intellectual dismissal.

literal_translation: dirt in your head / may dirt be on your head
english_equivalent: shame on you, you should be embarrassed
meaning: A variation of 'khāk bar saret' (already in database) — wishing dirt/earth on someone's head, invoking the burial imagery of death and shame. 'Tu' (in) instead of 'bar' (on) is slightly less formal but equally devastating. The image is of someone so shameful they should be buried.
cultural_context: In Persian mourning tradition, the bereaved literally throw dirt on their heads as a sign of grief. Wishing this on someone means they should be in mourning — either for their own dignity or as a death wish. The gesture of throwing dirt is viscerally understood by every Iranian, giving this phrase physical weight beyond its words.
example_usage: 'Khāk tu saret, in che kāri-e kardi?' — shame on you, what have you done? Said by parents, spouses, and friends about deeply embarrassing behavior.

meaning: 'Nang' is shame/disgrace — but not the internal feeling. It's the external mark of disgrace that others place on you. 'Nang-e khānevādeh' (disgrace of the family) is one of the most cutting phrases in Persian: you haven't just failed, you've stained everyone connected to you.
cultural_context: In collectivist Iranian culture, individual shame radiates outward to family, community, and even region. Being called 'nang' means your existence causes shame to others. It's the social version of a death sentence — you haven't just fallen, you've dragged everyone down with you. The concept is central to understanding Iranian honor dynamics.
example_usage: 'Nang-e khānevādeh hasti' — you're a disgrace to the family. 'Nang dāreh' — it brings shame. Said about behavior that reflects badly on the entire family.

meaning: 'Āberu' (literally 'water of the face' = reputation/dignity) + 'rizi' (spilling) = the act of losing face, causing embarrassment. It's not an insult directed at someone but a description of a shameful event. 'Āberu rizi kardi!' means 'you've embarrassed us/yourself!' The concept of 'āberu' is fundamental to Iranian social psychology.
cultural_context: Understanding 'āberu' unlocks Iranian social behavior. Much of ta'arof (ritualized politeness), family control of individual behavior, and even political dynamics revolve around protecting āberu. Spilling it — 'āberu rizi' — is one of the worst things that can happen socially. Iranians will go to extraordinary lengths to prevent it, which outsiders sometimes misread as dishonesty.
example_usage: 'Jolo-ye mardom āberu rizi kardi!' — you embarrassed us in front of people! 'Āberu-mun raft' — our reputation is gone. The universal Iranian social catastrophe.

meaning: A heavy, clumsy pack horse — applied to a person who's big, graceless, and not particularly bright. 'Yābu' is more about clumsiness than stupidity: you're not just dumb, you're physically awkward and ungainly. It's a gentler cousin of 'khar' (donkey), focusing on gracelessness rather than raw stupidity.
cultural_context: Iran's equestrian heritage gives it remarkably specific horse-based insults. A 'khar' (donkey) is stupid, an 'olāgh' is stubborn-stupid, a 'yābu' is clumsy-stupid, a 'kheng' is slow-stupid. Each animal and breed carries its own shade of inadequacy. It's like a sommelier of stupidity, with precise vocabulary for each variety.
example_usage: 'Mesle yābu rāh mireh' — he walks like a pack horse (clumsily). 'Yābu, zer nazan' — you oaf, stop talking nonsense.

meaning: Calling someone a monkey — implying ugly, foolish, or imitating without understanding. In Persian, 'meymun' insults can target appearance (ugly), behavior (clownish/imitative), or intelligence (mindlessly copying). 'Meymun sefat' (monkey-faced) is the appearance variant.
cultural_context: The monkey in Persian literary tradition represents thoughtless imitation — mimicking human behavior without understanding it. Calling someone 'meymun' often means they're a fake, a poser, someone who copies others without grasping what they're copying. This resonates in Iranian culture where authenticity ('asālat') is highly valued.
example_usage: 'Mesle meymun taqlid mikoneh' — he imitates like a monkey. 'Meymun, giyāfe-at-o bebīn!' — monkey, look at your face!

meaning: Calling someone a beggar — implying they're cheap, desperate, and without dignity. In a culture where generosity is a core virtue (Iranian hospitality is famous), being called 'gedā' attacks one of the most valued social qualities. It can mean literally poor but more often means stingy, mooching, or lacking self-respect.
cultural_context: Iranian hospitality culture ('mehmān navāzi') creates a specific horror around being perceived as a beggar or cheapskate. Iranians will insist on paying restaurant bills with theatrical intensity precisely because being seen as 'gedā' is social death. 'Gedā' as an insult targets this deep cultural anxiety around generosity and self-sufficiency.
example_usage: 'Gedā, har dafeh man bāyad hesāb konam?' — cheapskate, should I pay every time? 'Mesle gedā-hā raft-ār mikoneh' — he behaves like a beggar.

meaning: Stingy, miserly — someone who hoards money and refuses to share. In Iranian hospitality culture, being 'khasis' is a character flaw that borders on moral failure. The Arabic-origin word carries weight: it's not just 'careful with money,' it's a fundamental deficiency of generosity that makes someone socially repugnant.
cultural_context: Ta'arof demands performed generosity — you must offer, insist, and often provide. A 'khasis' person breaks this social contract and faces real consequences: fewer invitations, whispered criticism, social downgrade. In a culture where your reputation is your social currency, being known as 'khasis' is genuinely damaging.
example_usage: 'Adam-e khasisi-e, hich vaght dast tush nemireh' — he's a miser, he never reaches into his pocket. A serious character critique in Iranian social life.

meaning: A uniquely modern Tehran slang expression meaning 'destroyed, messed up, wrecked.' 'Dahan service' literally means 'mouth serviced' — the mouth has been so thoroughly shut/beaten that it's been 'serviced' like a broken machine. 'Dahan-am service shod' means 'I'm wrecked/exhausted/destroyed.'
cultural_context: This is pure Tehran street slang that emerged in the last 20-30 years and reflects Iran's distinctive ability to create euphemistic profanity that sounds technical. 'Service' (borrowed from French/English) applied to 'mouth' creates something absurdly mechanical. Young Tehranis love it for its creative indirectness — vulgar energy, clean vocabulary.
example_usage: 'Dahan-am service shod az in kār' — I'm wrecked from this work. 'Dahan service-esh kardam' — I destroyed him (verbally). Popular among under-40 Tehranis.

regional_variations.note: Spreading through social media and TV

meaning: Destroyed, fallen apart, wrecked — applied to things, situations, and people equally. 'Māshin-am dāghun shod' (my car got wrecked), 'dāghun-am' (I'm destroyed/exhausted), 'hameh chiz dāghun-e' (everything is messed up). It's one of Persian's most versatile expressions of damage.
cultural_context: Originally meant something physically demolished, but it's migrated into emotional and social territory. 'Dāghun shodam' after a breakup means emotional devastation. After a long day, it means exhaustion. The word's flexibility mirrors how Iranian emotional expression treats physical and emotional states as interchangeable.
example_usage: 'Dāghun-am, kheili kār kardam' — I'm wrecked, I worked too much. 'Zendegi-am dāghun shod' — my life got destroyed.

word: ریدم به این شانس (Ridam be in shāns)
english_equivalent: fuck this luck, damn my luck
meaning: A complete expression of existential frustration — literally shitting on one's own luck/fortune. It's the Persian equivalent of 'just my fucking luck,' expressed with characteristic scatological directness. The phrase combines 'ridan' (to shit) with the fatalism that pervades Persian culture.
cultural_context: Iranians have a deep relationship with luck, fate, and fortune — 'bakht,' 'shāns,' 'taqdeer' all represent forces beyond human control. Shitting on one's luck is a gesture of defiance against destiny itself. It's dramatic, it's vulgar, and it's a perfect example of how Persian profanity channels cosmic frustration into bodily metaphor.
example_usage: 'Ridam be in shāns, bāz ham bārāne!' — I shit on this luck, it's raining again! Said when misfortune feels personal and persistent.

meaning: Death — used in compound curses like 'marg bar X' (death to X). As a standalone or in compounds, it's the building block of Persian's death-wish expressions. 'Marg-et' means 'your death' — a curse. 'Marg bar dictator' became globally famous through Iranian political protests.
cultural_context: 'Marg bar...' (death to...) is Iran's most internationally recognized phrase, chanted at every political rally since the 1979 revolution. But in daily life, 'marg' appears in lighter contexts too: 'marg-am gereft' (death grabbed me = I was dying of embarrassment/laughter/fear). Persian uses 'death' with a casualness that foreign speakers find startling.
example_usage: Political: 'Marg bar...' (death to...). Personal: 'Az khejālat marg-am gereft' — I was dying of embarrassment. Both uses are extremely common.

meaning: The standard Persian 'damn' — from 'la'nat' (curse), adding '-i' to make it an adjective. 'In māshin-e la'nati' (this damned car), 'la'nati!' (damn it!). It's the moderate curse that fills the gap between mild expressions and serious profanity — strong enough to express real frustration, clean enough for most settings.
cultural_context: 'La'nat' has Quranic roots — God's curse on the disobedient. Using it as a casual exclamation technically invokes divine punishment, but like most religious-origin profanity, the theological weight has evaporated through overuse. It's one of Persian's most commonly uttered words, heard from taxi drivers to office workers to grandmothers.
example_usage: 'La'nati, bāz kharāb shod!' — damn it, it broke again! 'In ruz-e la'nati key tamum misheh?' — when will this damned day end?

meaning: An Arabic-origin word for excrement that functions as a more 'formal' version of 'goh' (shit). Calling something 'an' is calling it shit, but with an Arabic/literary register that 'goh' (pure Persian) lacks. 'An' is less common in speech than 'goh' but appears in certain set phrases and older expressions.
cultural_context: The Arabic/Persian dual vocabulary extends even to scatology: 'goh' (Persian) for everyday shit-talk, 'an' (Arabic) for when you want your scatological insults to sound educated. This parallel register system — crude in two languages — is one of Persian's most distinctive features.
example_usage: 'In ghazā an-e' — this food is shit. Less common than 'goh' in daily speech but understood everywhere. Used more by older speakers.

literal_translation: dog mother / mother is a dog
meaning: The maternal version of 'pedar sag' (dog father) — calling someone's mother a dog. In a culture where dogs are najis and mothers are sacred, this double violation is potent. It's slightly less common than 'pedar sag' but equally effective, attacking the maternal line instead of the paternal.
cultural_context: Persian insults can target either parent, but the dynamic differs: 'pedar sag' (father is a dog) attacks lineage and bloodline, while 'mādar sag' (mother is a dog) attacks the nurturer. In Iranian family structure, attacking the mother can feel more personal because of the emotional centrality of the mother figure in Iranian homes.
example_usage: 'Mādar sag, chi gofti?' — son of a bitch, what did you say? Used in confrontations and heated arguments.

meaning: A baby donkey — applied to someone young and foolish. 'Kore khar' is gentler than 'khar' alone because the 'kore' (foal/baby) adds a diminutive that softens the insult. It implies youthful stupidity — the target is not just dumb, they're immature and dumb. Often used by elders to scold younger people.
cultural_context: The pattern of 'baby animal' insults in Persian (tule sag for puppy, kore khar for donkey foal, gosaleh for calf) all carry the same implication: you're not only comparable to an animal, you're the baby version — doubly contemptible. 'Kore khar' is the mildest of these, used more for teasing than genuine anger.
example_usage: 'Kore khar, in chi kāri-e?' — you little jackass, what are you doing? Said by parents and older relatives to teenagers making dumb decisions.

meaning: Nonsense, rubbish talk, gibberish. 'Dari vari nagu!' means 'stop talking nonsense!' The word has a playful sound that makes it less aggressive than 'goh' (shit) or 'mozakhraf' (rubbish). It's the PG-rated way of saying 'you're talking bullshit' in Persian.
cultural_context: Etymology is debated — some connect it to 'dar' (door) suggesting door-to-door peddler's talk, others to 'Dari' (a variety of Persian) implying incomprehensible speech. Whatever its origin, 'dari vari' is a family-safe expression of contempt for someone's words. Grandparents use it freely.
example_usage: 'Dari vari nagu, rāst-esh-o begu!' — stop talking nonsense, tell the truth! 'Hame-ash dari vari-e' — it's all gibberish.

meaning: A rhyming compound meaning total nonsense. 'Chert' by itself means 'nonsense' or 'nap'; paired with 'pert' (which means nothing independently — it's there for rhyme) it creates a bouncy, dismissive expression. 'Chert o pert goftan' means 'to talk complete rubbish.'
cultural_context: Persian loves rhyming compounds where the second word exists only for sound: 'chert o pert,' 'āshghāl māshghāl,' 'kif o kuf.' These pairings are called 'esm-o-ism' patterns and they're a distinctive feature of colloquial Persian. 'Chert o pert' is among the most common — used in every social register from children to grandparents.
example_usage: 'Chert o pert nagu!' — don't talk rubbish! 'Tamām-esh chert o pert bud' — it was all nonsense (about a speech, article, or conversation).

meaning: Absurd, ridiculous, bullshit — from Arabic 'zakhraf' (false ornamentation). Something 'mozakhraf' isn't just wrong, it's elaborately, ornamentally wrong — dressed-up nonsense. It's the educated Persian's preferred word for calling bullshit because its Arabic literary register implies the speaker has intellectually evaluated and rejected what they heard.
cultural_context: The Arabic origin gives 'mozakhraf' an intellectual weight that pure Persian alternatives ('chert o pert,' 'dari vari') lack. Using it signals education and deliberate judgment. It's the word intellectuals, journalists, and politicians reach for. In Iranian media, 'mozakhraf' is the standard word for dismissing claims you consider beneath serious response.
example_usage: 'In harf-hā mozakhraf-e' — this talk is nonsense. 'Mozakhraf nagu!' — don't say absurd things! Commonly used in debates and intellectual arguments.

meaning: Talk that costs nothing — and is worth nothing. 'Harf-e moft nazan' means 'don't talk cheap.' It implies someone is making promises they won't keep or claims they can't support. The 'moft' (free/cheap) is the key: just as free things have no value, this speech has no substance.
cultural_context: In Iranian bazaar culture, where negotiation is an art form and words have economic weight, 'harf-e moft' is a specific insult: you're the kind of person whose word has no value in the marketplace of ideas and promises. It attacks credibility rather than intelligence — you might be smart, but your words are worthless.
example_usage: 'Harf-e moft nazan, āmal kon!' — stop with the cheap talk, do something! 'Hame-ash harf-e moft-e' — it's all empty words.

meaning: A liar — 'dorough' (lie) + 'gu' (sayer). In a culture built on ta'arof (where polite exaggeration is expected), being called an actual liar cuts through the social performance and says: this isn't ta'arof, you're genuinely dishonest. It's a character assault that matters in a society where personal reputation is everything.
cultural_context: The ta'arof system complicates lying in Persian culture. When your host says 'everything I have is yours,' they're not lying — they're performing ta'arof. But 'dorough-gu' breaks through this system to accuse someone of real, self-serving dishonesty. The distinction between socially mandated exaggeration and actual lying is crucial and 'dorough-gu' targets only the latter.
example_usage: 'Dorough-gu! Man khodam didam!' — liar! I saw it myself! 'Adam-e dorough-gu-yi-e' — he's a lying person (character judgment).

meaning: A euphemistic truncation of 'an mādar...' — 'that mother...' leaving the truly obscene part unsaid. Everyone knows what follows ('jendeh' or 'ghahbeh'), but by stopping short, the speaker maintains a thin veneer of deniability. It's the Persian equivalent of saying 'son of a—' and stopping.
cultural_context: Persian's rich euphemism system allows speakers to invoke nuclear insults while technically not saying them. 'An mādar' is understood as clearly as the full phrase, but the truncation allows use in semi-public settings. Iranian TV censors ban the full phrases but 'an mādar' slips through because it's technically incomplete. Everyone knows. Nobody says.
example_usage: 'An mādar...!' — that mother—! Said with a trailing off that communicates everything. Used when full profanity would be socially impossible but the emotion demands expression.

meaning: Technically not an insult — 'jiger' (liver) is slang for an attractive person. 'Che jigeri!' means 'what a babe!' But in street culture, catcalling someone 'jiger' is crude objectification. The word occupies a fascinating space between compliment and harassment depending entirely on context and consent.
cultural_context: Why liver? In classical Persian poetry and medicine, the liver (not the heart) was the seat of passion and desire. 'Jiger' as a compliment for beauty comes directly from this tradition — calling someone your liver means they inflame your desire. Modern usage has reduced this poetic tradition to catcalling, which is both linguistically interesting and socially problematic.
example_usage: Between partners: 'Jiger-am' — my liver/my darling. Street catcalling: 'Che jigeri!' — what a babe! The context makes it either endearing or offensive.

meaning: A generalized version of 'khāk bar saret' (already in database) — used as an exclamation about situations rather than directed at a specific person. 'Khāk bar sar-e in zendegi' (dirt on this life's head) expresses existential frustration. It transforms the personal curse into a general expression of despair.
cultural_context: The mourning gesture of throwing dirt on one's head has become so embedded in Persian expression that it can be applied to abstract concepts. You can put dirt on life's head, on luck's head, on a situation's head. The original ritual meaning has been abstracted into a universal expression of 'this is terrible and I'm in mourning about it.'
example_usage: 'Khāk bar sar-e in roozgār' — damn this era/times. 'Khāk bar sar' muttered about any frustrating situation. More existential than personal.
